chunks.text: Baroness Keeley Chair of the Communications and Digital Committee House of Lords London SW1A 0PW Email: holcommunications@parliament. uk Dame Melanie Dawes Chief Executive Ofcom Riverside House 2A Southwark Bridge Road London SE1 9HA 31 October 2025 DearDame Melanie, Thank you for coming to give evidence to the Communications and Digital Committee on 14 October. We welcomed the opportunity to discuss with you and Mark Bunting topics that we have been examining in recent months, particularly Ofcom’s implementation of the Online Safety Act (OSA). Ofcom has described 2025 as its “year of action” on online safety. The Committee acknowledges the significant amount of work that was undertaken to bring the illegal content and protection of children codes in to effect earlier this year. We recognise toothatweare still in the early days of implementation and en for cement, and that further monitoring will be needed to assess fully the impact of the new duties. We will review with interest the findings on impact set out in Ofcom’s forthcoming annual report and the review ofplatforms’ risk assessments that you referenced during our evidence session. In September, we took evidence from academics and civil society organisations regarding Ofcom’s additional safety measures consultation, which closed on 20 October. While we support Ofcom’sefforts to address gaps in the first-edition version of the codes, we are concerned that in some are as the proposed new measures donotgofar enough. We heard, for example, that there is a “loophole” in the proposed new safety measures relating to livestreaming by children, since these would apply only to ‘one-to-many’ livestreams and not ‘many-to-many’. We also heard the suggestion that a minimum age of 16 could be introduced for hosting, in recognition of the particular risk that younger children face. It is vital that Ofcom takes note of the expert insight it has received during the recent Communications and Digital Committee, 14 October 2025 - Ofcom leadership - Oral evidence Ofcom, Consultation: Online Safety - Additional Safety Measures , 30 June 2025

In September, we took evidence from academics and civil society organisations regarding Ofcom’s additional safety measures consultation, which closed on 20 October. We also heard the suggestion that a minimum age of 16 could be introduced for hosting, in recognition of the particular risk that younger children face. It is vital that Ofcom takes note of the expert insight it has received during the recent Communications and Digital Committee, 14 October 2025 - Ofcom leadership - Oral evidence Ofcom, Consultation: Online Safety - Additional Safety Measures , 30 June 2025

chunks.text: consultation on this and other matters. Relevant measures must be streng the ned where there is clear evidence of harm. We welcome your indication that you will keep provisions on livestreaming by children under review and “go further” than what is currently proposed if necessary. Discussing the implementation of the OSA more broadly, witnesses described Ofcom’s approach as “gradualist” and “incredibly slow”. We heard thatplatforms are not “quaking in their boots” with regard to en for cement. While it is positive tohearthaten for cement activity is a lready underway, we urgeyoutogo further and faster where possible. In particular, in response to this letter, please set out the actions you will take in relation to service providers that fail to engage with your en for cement work. During our session, I raised with you reports that users had encountered appalling antisemitic abuse on social media following the horrific He at on Park Synagogue attack. You told us that Ofcom had “followed up” with the large social media platforms to understand the steps they took in response to this event and their preparedness for managing the risk of illegal activity on their services. What reassurances have you received fromplatforms regarding their response to the 2 October attack? Howwillany learnings regarding the effectiveness of such responses be fed in to subsequent considerations on the proposed new requirement for crisis response protocols put forward in your recent consultation? We look forward to your response on these matters, in addition to the further in for mation on your roadmap for OSA implementation and methods to detect the use of virtual private networks (VPNs) that you agreed to provide during our session. We will also be interested to see a breakdown on the purposes of use of VPNs by under-18swhenthisdata is a vailable. Please note that we intend to place this letter, and your response, in the public domain. Yours sincerely, Baroness Keeley QQ 39, 46 (Andy Burrows, Baroness Kidron) Ofcom, Ofcom issues update on Online Safety Act investigations , 13 October 2025
